    --volume-id vol-0abc123def456789 \
    --description "anihpj DB backup before JobPost migration" \
    --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=snapshot,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-db-backup-20250117},{Key=Type,Value=pre-migration}]'
Output: { "SnapshotId": "snap-0abc123snapshot", "VolumeId": "vol-0abc123def456789", "State": "pending", "VolumeSize": 100, "StartTime": "2025-01-17T10:30:00.000Z", "Description": "anihpj DB backup before JobPost migration", "Encrypted": true, "Tags": [ {"Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-db-backup-20250117"}, {"Key": "Type", "Value": "pre-migration"} ] }
Django Context: # Backup created before running: # python manage.py migrate jobpost # Preserves: Skills, Author, Location, User, JobPost data # Can restore if migration fails
Example 4: Describe volumes

List all EBS volumes for the Django application.

aws ec2 describe-volumes \
    --filters "Name=tag:App,Values=jobpost" \
    --query "Volumes[*].{ID:VolumeId,Size:Size,Type:VolumeType,State:State,AZ:AvailabilityZone}"
Output: [ { "ID": "vol-0abc123def456789", "Size": 100, "Type": "gp3", "State": "in-use", "AZ": "us-east-1a" }, { "ID": "vol-0xyz789backup", "Size": 50, "Type": "gp3", "State": "available", "AZ": "us-east-1a" } ]
Django Context: # Volumes support Django operations: # - vol-0abc123: Primary DB for JobPost queries # - vol-0xyz789: Backup/staging database # - in-use attached to Django EC2 instance
Example 5: Modify volume size

Increase volume size as JobPost data grows.

aws ec2 modify-volume \
    --volume-id vol-0abc123def456789 \
    --size 200 \
    --volume-type gp3 \
    --iops 6000 \
    --throughput 250
Output: { "VolumeModification": { "VolumeId": "vol-0abc123def456789", "ModificationState": "modifying", "TargetSize": 200, "TargetVolumeType": "gp3", "TargetIops": 6000, "TargetThroughput": 250, "OriginalSize": 100, "OriginalVolumeType": "gp3", "OriginalIops": 3000, "OriginalThroughput": 125, "Progress": 0, "StartTime": "2025-01-17T11:00:00.000Z" } }
Django Context: # Expanded for growing data: # JobPost.objects.count() exceeding capacity # Skills.objects.all() - more skill entries # Author profiles with larger media # Double IOPS for faster queries

🛠️ Developer Tools

AWS Developer Tools provide comprehensive CI/CD capabilities for automating your Django application deployment pipeline. This section covers CodePipeline for orchestration, CodeBuild for compilation, CodeDeploy for deployment, and CodeCommit for source control.

AWS CodePipeline

Continuous delivery service for fast and reliable updates

📋 CodePipeline Overview

AWS CodePipeline is a continuous delivery service that automates build, test, and deploy phases of your release process. For the anihpj Django application, CodePipeline orchestrates the deployment of JobPost features, runs tests for Skills validation, and deploys updates to production environments.

Core CodePipeline Commands

create-pipeline - Create new pipeline
start-pipeline-execution - Trigger pipeline
get-pipeline-state - Get current state
list-pipelines - List all pipelines
update-pipeline - Update pipeline config
get-pipeline-execution - Get execution details

create-pipeline

Creates a new deployment pipeline for Django application.

Syntax
aws codepipeline create-pipeline \
    --pipeline file://<pipeline-definition.json>
Example 1: Create Django deployment pipeline

Create a pipeline for anihpj Django application.

# pipeline-definition.json
{
    "pipeline": {
        "name": "anihpj-django-pipeline",
        "roleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CodePipelineRole",
        "stages": [
            {
                "name": "Source",
                "actions": [{
                    "name": "SourceAction",
                    "actionTypeId": {
                        "category": "Source",
                        "owner": "AWS",
                        "provider": "CodeCommit",
                        "version": "1"
                    },
                    "configuration": {
                        "RepositoryName": "anihpj-django",
                        "BranchName": "main"
                    },
                    "outputArtifacts": [{"name": "SourceOutput"}]
                }]
            },
            {
                "name": "Build",
                "actions": [{
                    "name": "BuildAction",
                    "actionTypeId": {
                        "category": "Build",
                        "owner": "AWS",
                        "provider": "CodeBuild",
                        "version": "1"
                    },
                    "inputArtifacts": [{"name": "SourceOutput"}],
                    "outputArtifacts": [{"name": "BuildOutput"}],
                    "configuration": {
                        "ProjectName": "anihpj-build"
                    }
                }]
            },
            {
                "name": "Deploy",
                "actions": [{
                    "name": "DeployAction",
                    "actionTypeId": {
                        "category": "Deploy",
                        "owner": "AWS",
                        "provider": "ElasticBeanstalk",
                        "version": "1"
                    },
                    "inputArtifacts": [{"name": "BuildOutput"}],
                    "configuration": {
                        "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost",
                        "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-prod"
                    }
                }]
            }
        ]
    }
}

aws codepipeline create-pipeline --pipeline file://pipeline-definition.json
Output: { "pipeline": { "name": "anihpj-django-pipeline", "roleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CodePipelineRole", "stages": [...], "version": 1 } }
Django Context: # Pipeline deploys Django with models: # - JobPost CRUD operations # - Skills management # - Author/Location updates # Auto-deploys on git push to main

start-pipeline-execution

Starts the pipeline execution manually.

Example 2: Trigger deployment manually

Start a new deployment for Django application.

aws codepipeline start-pipeline-execution \
    --name anihpj-django-pipeline
Output: { "pipelineExecutionId": "exec-0abc123def456" }
Django Context: # Deploys latest Django code with: # - New JobPost fields # - Skills model updates # - Author relationship changes
Example 3: Get pipeline state

Check current status of the deployment pipeline.

aws codepipeline get-pipeline-state \
    --name anihpj-django-pipeline
Output: { "pipelineName": "anihpj-django-pipeline", "pipelineVersion": 1, "stageStates": [ { "stageName": "Source", "latestExecution": { "status": "Succeeded" } }, { "stageName": "Build", "latestExecution": { "status": "Succeeded" } }, { "stageName": "Deploy", "latestExecution": { "status": "InProgress" } } ] }
Django Context: # Deployment progress: # - Source: Code fetched ✓ # - Build: Tests passed ✓ # - Deploy: Updating Elastic Beanstalk...
Example 4: List all pipelines

Get list of all deployment pipelines.

aws codepipeline list-pipelines
Output: { "pipelines": [ { "name": "anihpj-django-pipeline", "version": 1, "created": "2025-01-17T10:00:00.000Z", "updated": "2025-01-17T12:30:00.000Z" }, { "name": "anihpj-staging-pipeline", "version": 2, "created": "2025-01-15T09:00:00.000Z", "updated": "2025-01-16T14:00:00.000Z" } ] }
Django Context: # Pipelines for different environments: # - anihpj-django-pipeline: Production # - anihpj-staging-pipeline: Testing # Both deploy JobPost application
Example 5: Get execution details

Get detailed information about a specific execution.

aws codepipeline get-pipeline-execution \
    --pipeline-name anihpj-django-pipeline \
    --pipeline-execution-id exec-0abc123def456
Output: { "pipelineExecution": { "pipelineName": "anihpj-django-pipeline", "pipelineVersion": 1, "pipelineExecutionId": "exec-0abc123def456", "status": "Succeeded", "artifactRevisions": [ { "name": "SourceOutput", "revisionId": "abc123commit", "revisionSummary": "feat: Add Skills.level field to JobPost", "revisionUrl": "https://git-codecommit.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/v1/repos/anihpj-django/commit/abc123" } ] } }
Django Context: # Deployed commit details: # - Added Skills.level field # - JobPost.required_skills now includes level # - Commit abc123 is live in production

AWS CodeBuild

Fully managed build service for compiling and testing code

📋 CodeBuild Overview

AWS CodeBuild is a fully managed continuous integration service that compiles source code, runs tests, and produces software packages. For the anihpj Django application, CodeBuild runs pytest tests for JobPost, Skills, Author, Location, and User models, builds Docker images, and packages the application for deployment.

Core CodeBuild Commands

create-project - Create build project
start-build - Start a build
batch-get-builds - Get build details
list-projects - List projects
list-builds-for-project - List builds
stop-build - Stop running build

create-project

Creates a new CodeBuild project for Django application.

Syntax
aws codebuild create-project \
    --name <project-name> \
    --source <source-config> \
    --environment <environment-config> \
    --service-role <role-arn> \
    --artifacts <artifacts-config>
Example 1: Create Django build project

Create a CodeBuild project for testing and building.

aws codebuild create-project \
    --name anihpj-build \
    --source type=CODECOMMIT,location=https://git-codecommit.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/v1/repos/anihpj-django \
    --environment type=LINUX_CONTAINER,computeType=BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL,image=aws/codebuild/amazonlinux2-x86_64-standard:5.0,environmentVariables=[{name=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE,value=anihpj.settings.testing}] \
    --service-role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CodeBuildRole \
    --artifacts type=S3,location=anihpj-build-artifacts
Output: { "project": { "name": "anihpj-build", "arn": "arn:aws:codebuild:us-east-1:123456789012:project/anihpj-build", "source": { "type": "CODECOMMIT", "location": "https://git-codecommit.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/v1/repos/anihpj-django" }, "environment": { "type": "LINUX_CONTAINER", "computeType": "BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL", "image": "aws/codebuild/amazonlinux2-x86_64-standard:5.0" }, "created": "2025-01-17T10:00:00.000Z" } }
Django Context: # Build project runs: # - pip install -r requirements.txt # - pytest tests/ (JobPost, Skills tests) # - python manage.py collectstatic

start-build

Starts a new build for the project.

Example 2: Start build with environment override

Start a build with custom environment variables.

aws codebuild start-build \
    --project-name anihpj-build \
    --environment-variables-override name=RUN_INTEGRATION_TESTS,value=true name=DATABASE_URL,value=postgres://test:test@localhost/test
Output: { "build": { "id": "anihpj-build:abc123-build-id", "arn": "arn:aws:codebuild:us-east-1:123456789012:build/anihpj-build:abc123-build-id", "buildNumber": 42, "startTime": "2025-01-17T10:15:00.000Z", "currentPhase": "SUBMITTED", "buildStatus": "IN_PROGRESS", "initiator": "user/developer" } }
Django Context: # Build runs integration tests: # - JobPost.objects.create() tests # - Skills.objects.filter() tests # - Author relationship tests
Example 3: Get build details

Retrieve details of a specific build.

aws codebuild batch-get-builds \
    --ids anihpj-build:abc123-build-id
Output: { "builds": [ { "id": "anihpj-build:abc123-build-id", "buildNumber": 42, "buildStatus": "SUCCEEDED", "startTime": "2025-01-17T10:15:00.000Z", "endTime": "2025-01-17T10:18:30.000Z", "phases": [ {"phaseType": "SUBMITTED", "phaseStatus": "SUCCEEDED", "durationInSeconds": 1}, {"phaseType": "PROVISIONING", "phaseStatus": "SUCCEEDED", "durationInSeconds": 25}, {"phaseType": "DOWNLOAD_SOURCE", "phaseStatus": "SUCCEEDED", "durationInSeconds": 8}, {"phaseType": "INSTALL", "phaseStatus": "SUCCEEDED", "durationInSeconds": 45}, {"phaseType": "PRE_BUILD", "phaseStatus": "SUCCEEDED", "durationInSeconds": 5}, {"phaseType": "BUILD", "phaseStatus": "SUCCEEDED", "durationInSeconds": 120}, {"phaseType": "POST_BUILD", "phaseStatus": "SUCCEEDED", "durationInSeconds": 10} ], "logs": { "groupName": "/aws/codebuild/anihpj-build", "streamName": "abc123-build-id" } } ] }
Django Context: # Build succeeded in 3.5 minutes: # - All pytest tests passed # - Django migrations validated # - Static files collected
Example 4: buildspec.yml for Django

CodeBuild buildspec file for Django project.

# buildspec.yml
version: 0.2

phases:
  install:
    runtime-versions:
      python: 3.11
    commands:
      - pip install --upgrade pip
      - pip install -r requirements.txt
  
  pre_build:
    commands:
      - echo "Running Django checks..."
      - python manage.py check
      - echo "Running migrations check..."
      - python manage.py migrate --check
  
  build:
    commands:
      - echo "Running pytest..."
      - pytest tests/ -v --cov=jobpost --cov-report=xml
      - echo "Collecting static files..."
      - python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
  
  post_build:
    commands:
      - echo "Build completed on $(date)"

artifacts:
  files:
    - '**/*'
  exclude-paths:
    - '.git/**/*'
    - '__pycache__/**/*'

reports:
  pytest-reports:
    files:
      - 'coverage.xml'
    file-format: COBERTURAXML
Output: # Build logs show: [Container] Running command pytest tests/ -v --cov=jobpost ======================== test session starts ======================== tests/test_models.py::TestJobPost::test_create PASSED tests/test_models.py::TestSkills::test_filter PASSED tests/test_models.py::TestAuthor::test_relationship PASSED ======================== 45 passed in 12.34s ========================
Django Context: # pytest validates: # - JobPost.objects.create() works # - Skills.objects.filter() returns correct data # - Author.objects.select_related() optimized
Example 5: List builds for project

Get recent builds for the Django project.

aws codebuild list-builds-for-project \
    --project-name anihpj-build \
    --sort-order DESCENDING
Output: { "ids": [ "anihpj-build:abc123-build-id", "anihpj-build:xyz789-build-id", "anihpj-build:def456-build-id", "anihpj-build:ghi012-build-id", "anihpj-build:jkl345-build-id" ] }
Django Context: # Recent builds show deployment history: # - Latest: JobPost status field added # - Previous: Skills level validation # - Older: Author bio field migration

AWS CodeDeploy

Automated deployment service for EC2, Lambda, and ECS

📋 CodeDeploy Overview

AWS CodeDeploy automates software deployments to EC2 instances, on-premises servers, Lambda functions, or ECS services. For the anihpj Django application, CodeDeploy handles zero-downtime deployments, rolling updates, and automatic rollback if deployment fails.

Core CodeDeploy Commands

create-application - Create application
create-deployment-group - Create deployment group
create-deployment - Start deployment
get-deployment - Get deployment status
list-deployments - List deployments
stop-deployment - Stop deployment

create-application

Creates a new CodeDeploy application.

Syntax
aws deploy create-application \
    --application-name <name> \
    --compute-platform Server|Lambda|ECS
Example 1: Create EC2 deployment application

Create a CodeDeploy application for Django EC2 instances.

aws deploy create-application \
    --application-name anihpj-django-app \
    --compute-platform Server \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Framework,Value=Django
Output: { "applicationId": "app-0abc123def456" }
Django Context: # Application for deploying Django to EC2: # - Serves JobPost API endpoints # - Handles Skills, Author, Location queries # - Runs on multiple EC2 instances

create-deployment-group

Creates a deployment group for the application.

Example 2: Create production deployment group

Create deployment group for production Django servers.

aws deploy create-deployment-group \
    --application-name anihpj-django-app \
    --deployment-group-name anihpj-prod-group \
    --deployment-config-name CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime \
    --ec2-tag-filters Key=Environment,Value=production,Type=KEY_AND_VALUE Key=App,Value=anihpj,Type=KEY_AND_VALUE \
    --service-role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CodeDeployRole \
    --auto-rollback-configuration enabled=true,events=DEPLOYMENT_FAILURE,DEPLOYMENT_STOP_ON_REQUEST
Output: { "deploymentGroupId": "dg-0abc123prod" }
Django Context: # Production group deploys to: # - EC2 instances tagged Environment=production # - One instance at a time (zero downtime) # - Auto-rollback on failure

create-deployment

Starts a new deployment.

Example 3: Deploy from S3

Deploy Django application from S3 artifact.

aws deploy create-deployment \
    --application-name anihpj-django-app \
    --deployment-group-name anihpj-prod-group \
    --s3-location bucket=anihpj-deployments,key=releases/v2.1.0.zip,bundleType=zip \
    --description "Deploy v2.1.0 - Added Skills.level field"
Output: { "deploymentId": "d-ABC123XYZ" }
Django Context: # Deploying Django v2.1.0: # - New Skills.level field (beginner, intermediate, advanced) # - JobPost.required_skills now includes skill levels # - Updated querysets throughout
Example 4: Get deployment status

Check the status of ongoing deployment.

aws deploy get-deployment \
    --deployment-id d-ABC123XYZ
Output: { "deploymentInfo": { "applicationName": "anihpj-django-app", "deploymentGroupName": "anihpj-prod-group", "deploymentId": "d-ABC123XYZ", "status": "Succeeded", "createTime": "2025-01-17T10:00:00.000Z", "completeTime": "2025-01-17T10:15:00.000Z", "deploymentOverview": { "Pending": 0, "InProgress": 0, "Succeeded": 3, "Failed": 0, "Skipped": 0, "Ready": 0 }, "description": "Deploy v2.1.0 - Added Skills.level field", "revision": { "revisionType": "S3", "s3Location": { "bucket": "anihpj-deployments", "key": "releases/v2.1.0.zip" } } } }
Django Context: # Deployment completed on 3 instances: # - All serving updated JobPost API # - Skills.objects.filter(level='advanced') works # - Zero downtime achieved
Example 5: appspec.yml for Django

CodeDeploy appspec.yml for Django deployment.

# appspec.yml
version: 0.0
os: linux
files:
  - source: /
    destination: /var/www/anihpj

hooks:
  BeforeInstall:
    - location: scripts/before_install.sh
      timeout: 300
      runas: root
  
  AfterInstall:
    - location: scripts/after_install.sh
      timeout: 300
      runas: root
  
  ApplicationStart:
    - location: scripts/start_server.sh
      timeout: 60
      runas: www-data
  
  ValidateService:
    - location: scripts/validate.sh
      timeout: 60
      runas: www-data

# scripts/after_install.sh
#!/bin/bash
cd /var/www/anihpj
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
python manage.py migrate --noinput
python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# scripts/validate.sh
#!/bin/bash
curl -f http://localhost:8000/api/health/ || exit 1
Output: # Deployment lifecycle: [stdout] Installing dependencies... [stdout] Running migrations... Operations to perform: Apply all migrations: jobpost, auth, contenttypes Running migrations: Applying jobpost.0015_skills_level... OK [stdout] Validating service... [stdout] Health check passed!
Django Context: # Deployment runs Django migrations: # - Skills.level field added # - JobPost model unchanged # - Health check validates API works

AWS CodeCommit

Managed source control service for Git repositories

📋 CodeCommit Overview

AWS CodeCommit is a fully managed source control service that hosts Git repositories. For the anihpj Django application, CodeCommit stores the application code, tracks changes to JobPost, Skills, Author, Location, and User models, and integrates with CodePipeline for automated deployments.

Core CodeCommit Commands

create-repository - Create new repo
get-repository - Get repo details
list-repositories - List all repos
create-branch - Create branch
get-branch - Get branch info
create-pull-request - Create PR

create-repository

Creates a new Git repository in CodeCommit.

Syntax
aws codecommit create-repository \
    --repository-name <name> \
    --repository-description <description> \
    [--tags Key=<key>,Value=<value>]
Example 1: Create Django repository

Create a CodeCommit repository for the Django project.

aws codecommit create-repository \
    --repository-name anihpj-django \
    --repository-description "Django job posting application with Skills, Author, Location, User, JobPost models" \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Framework,Value=Django
Output: { "repositoryMetadata": { "accountId": "123456789012", "repositoryId": "repo-abc123def456", "repositoryName": "anihpj-django", "repositoryDescription": "Django job posting application with Skills, Author, Location, User, JobPost models", "cloneUrlHttp": "https://git-codecommit.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/v1/repos/anihpj-django", "cloneUrlSsh": "ssh://git-codecommit.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/v1/repos/anihpj-django", "creationDate": "2025-01-17T10:00:00.000Z", "Arn": "arn:aws:codecommit:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-django" } }
Django Context: # Repository stores Django project: # - jobpost/models.py (Skills, Author, Location, User, JobPost) # - jobpost/views.py (API endpoints) # - tests/ (pytest test files)

create-branch

Creates a new branch in the repository.

Example 2: Create feature branch

Create a branch for new JobPost feature.

aws codecommit create-branch \
    --repository-name anihpj-django \
    --branch-name feature/jobpost-status \
    --commit-id abc123def456789
Output: # No output on success (HTTP 200)
Django Context: # Feature branch for: # - Adding JobPost.status field (draft, published, archived) # - Updating JobPost.objects.filter(status='published') # - New admin filters for status
Example 3: List branches

List all branches in the repository.

aws codecommit list-branches \
    --repository-name anihpj-django
Output: { "branches": [ "main", "develop", "feature/jobpost-status", "feature/skills-level", "hotfix/author-validation" ] }
Django Context: # Branches for different features: # - main: Production code # - feature/jobpost-status: JobPost workflow # - feature/skills-level: Skills.level field # - hotfix/author-validation: Author.bio validation fix

create-pull-request

Creates a pull request for code review.

Example 4: Create pull request

Create a PR for the JobPost status feature.

aws codecommit create-pull-request \
    --title "Add JobPost status field" \
    --description "Adds status field (draft, published, archived) to JobPost model. Updates querysets and admin filters." \
    --targets repositoryName=anihpj-django,sourceReference=feature/jobpost-status,destinationReference=main
Output: { "pullRequest": { "pullRequestId": "123", "title": "Add JobPost status field", "description": "Adds status field (draft, published, archived) to JobPost model. Updates querysets and admin filters.", "pullRequestStatus": "OPEN", "creationDate": "2025-01-17T10:30:00.000Z", "pullRequestTargets": [ { "repositoryName": "anihpj-django", "sourceReference": "refs/heads/feature/jobpost-status", "destinationReference": "refs/heads/main", "mergeMetadata": { "isMerged": false } } ] } }
Django Context: # PR changes include: # - models.py: JobPost.status = CharField(choices=STATUS_CHOICES) # - views.py: JobPost.objects.filter(status='published') # - admin.py: list_filter = ['status'] # - migrations/0015_jobpost_status.py
Example 5: Get commit history

Get recent commits for the Django repository.

aws codecommit get-branch \
    --repository-name anihpj-django \
    --branch-name main

aws codecommit get-commit \
    --repository-name anihpj-django \
    --commit-id abc123def456789
Output: { "branch": { "branchName": "main", "commitId": "abc123def456789" } } { "commit": { "commitId": "abc123def456789", "treeId": "tree123abc", "parents": ["parent789xyz"], "message": "feat: Add Skills.level field for skill proficiency\n\n- Added level field to Skills model\n- Updated JobPost.required_skills querysets\n- Added migration 0014_skills_level", "author": { "name": "Django Developer", "email": "dev@anihpj.com", "date": "2025-01-17T09:00:00.000Z" }, "committer": { "name": "Django Developer", "email": "dev@anihpj.com", "date": "2025-01-17T09:00:00.000Z" } } }
Django Context: # Commit adds Skills.level: # Skills.objects.filter(level='advanced') # JobPost.required_skills.filter(level__gte='intermediate') # Used in matching jobs to user skills

⚙️ Management & Governance

AWS Management services help you monitor, manage, and govern your infrastructure. This section covers CloudWatch for monitoring, CloudFormation for infrastructure as code, Systems Manager for operational management, and CloudTrail for auditing.

AWS CloudWatch

Monitoring and observability service for AWS resources

📋 CloudWatch Overview

AWS CloudWatch provides monitoring and observability for AWS resources and applications. For the anihpj Django application, CloudWatch monitors API response times, tracks JobPost creation rates, alerts on error spikes, and provides dashboards for Skills search performance and Author activity metrics.

Core CloudWatch Commands

put-metric-data - Publish custom metrics
get-metric-statistics - Get metric data
put-metric-alarm - Create alarm
describe-alarms - List alarms
put-dashboard - Create dashboard
get-log-events - Get log entries

put-metric-data

Publishes custom metric data to CloudWatch.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
    --namespace <namespace> \
    --metric-name <name> \
    --value <value> \
    [--unit <unit>] \
    [--dimensions Name=<name>,Value=<value>]
Example 1: Track JobPost creation count

Publish custom metric for JobPost creations.

aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
    --namespace "Anihpj/Django" \
    --metric-name "JobPostCreations" \
    --value 1 \
    --unit Count \
    --dimensions Name=Environment,Value=production Name=Model,Value=JobPost
Output: # No output on success (HTTP 200)
Django Context: # In Django signal: # @receiver(post_save, sender=JobPost) # def track_jobpost_creation(sender, instance, created, **kwargs): # if created: # cloudwatch.put_metric_data(...)

put-metric-alarm

Creates or updates a CloudWatch alarm.

Example 2: Create high error rate alarm

Alert when Django API errors exceed threshold.

aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
    --alarm-name anihpj-high-error-rate \
    --alarm-description "Alert when Django 5xx errors exceed 10/min" \
    --metric-name 5XXError \
    --namespace "AWS/ApplicationELB" \
    --statistic Sum \
    --period 60 \
    --threshold 10 \
    --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
    --evaluation-periods 2 \
    --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-alerts \
    --dimensions Name=LoadBalancer,Value=app/anihpj-lb/1234567890
Output: # No output on success (HTTP 200)
Django Context: # Monitors Django API errors: # - JobPost.DoesNotExist exceptions # - Skills validation errors # - Author permission denied errors
Example 3: Get API latency statistics

Retrieve Django API response time metrics.

aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
    --namespace "AWS/ApplicationELB" \
    --metric-name TargetResponseTime \
    --start-time 2025-01-17T00:00:00Z \
    --end-time 2025-01-17T12:00:00Z \
    --period 3600 \
    --statistics Average Maximum \
    --dimensions Name=LoadBalancer,Value=app/anihpj-lb/1234567890
Output: { "Label": "TargetResponseTime", "Datapoints": [ { "Timestamp": "2025-01-17T08:00:00Z", "Average": 0.045, "Maximum": 0.125, "Unit": "Seconds" }, { "Timestamp": "2025-01-17T09:00:00Z", "Average": 0.052, "Maximum": 0.189, "Unit": "Seconds" }, { "Timestamp": "2025-01-17T10:00:00Z", "Average": 0.048, "Maximum": 0.156, "Unit": "Seconds" } ] }
Django Context: # Response times for Django views: # - Average 45-52ms for JobPost.objects.all() # - Max 189ms for complex Skills filter queries # - Optimized with select_related()
Example 4: Describe active alarms

List all CloudWatch alarms for anihpj.

aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
    --alarm-name-prefix anihpj
Output: { "MetricAlarms": [ { "AlarmName": "anihpj-high-error-rate", "AlarmDescription": "Alert when Django 5xx errors exceed 10/min", "StateValue": "OK", "MetricName": "5XXError", "Threshold": 10.0, "ComparisonOperator": "GreaterThanThreshold" }, { "AlarmName": "anihpj-high-latency", "AlarmDescription": "Alert when API latency exceeds 500ms", "StateValue": "OK", "MetricName": "TargetResponseTime", "Threshold": 0.5, "ComparisonOperator": "GreaterThanThreshold" }, { "AlarmName": "anihpj-low-healthy-hosts", "AlarmDescription": "Alert when healthy Django instances below 2", "StateValue": "OK", "MetricName": "HealthyHostCount", "Threshold": 2.0, "ComparisonOperator": "LessThanThreshold" } ] }
Django Context: # Alarms monitor: # - API errors (JobPost validation, Auth failures) # - Response time (QuerySet performance) # - Instance health (Django server availability)
Example 5: Create monitoring dashboard

Create a CloudWatch dashboard for Django metrics.

aws cloudwatch put-dashboard \
    --dashboard-name anihpj-django-dashboard \
    --dashboard-body '{
        "widgets": [
            {
                "type": "metric",
                "x": 0, "y": 0, "width": 12, "height": 6,
                "properties": {
                    "title": "JobPost API Requests",
                    "metrics": [
                        ["AWS/ApplicationELB", "RequestCount", "LoadBalancer", "app/anihpj-lb/1234567890"]
                    ],
                    "period": 60,
                    "stat": "Sum"
                }
            },
            {
                "type": "metric",
                "x": 12, "y": 0, "width": 12, "height": 6,
                "properties": {
                    "title": "API Response Time",
                    "metrics": [
                        ["AWS/ApplicationELB", "TargetResponseTime", "LoadBalancer", "app/anihpj-lb/1234567890"]
                    ],
                    "period": 60,
                    "stat": "Average"
                }
            },
            {
                "type": "metric",
                "x": 0, "y": 6, "width": 24, "height": 6,
                "properties": {
                    "title": "Custom Django Metrics",
                    "metrics": [
                        ["Anihpj/Django", "JobPostCreations", "Environment", "production"],
                        ["Anihpj/Django", "SkillsSearches", "Environment", "production"]
                    ],
                    "period": 300,
                    "stat": "Sum"
                }
            }
        ]
    }'
Output: { "DashboardValidationMessages": [] }
Django Context: # Dashboard displays: # - JobPost API request volume # - QuerySet response times # - Custom metrics for JobPost.objects.create() # - Skills.objects.filter() search counts

🏗️ AWS CloudFormation

Infrastructure as Code for provisioning AWS resources

📋 CloudFormation Overview

AWS CloudFormation lets you model and provision AWS resources using templates. For the anihpj Django application, CloudFormation defines the complete infrastructure stack including VPC, RDS database, EC2/ECS instances, load balancers, and S3 buckets needed to run the JobPost application.

Core CloudFormation Commands

create-stack - Create new stack
update-stack - Update existing stack
describe-stacks - Get stack info
describe-stack-events - Get stack events
delete-stack - Delete stack
validate-template - Validate template

create-stack

Creates a new CloudFormation stack from a template.

Syntax
aws cloudformation create-stack \
    --stack-name <name> \
    --template-body file://<template.yaml> \
    [--parameters ParameterKey=<key>,ParameterValue=<value>] \
    [--capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM]
Example 1: Create Django infrastructure stack

Deploy complete infrastructure for anihpj Django application.

aws cloudformation create-stack \
    --stack-name anihpj-production \
    --template-body file://infrastructure.yaml \
    --parameters \
        ParameterKey=Environment,ParameterValue=production \
        ParameterKey=DBPassword,ParameterValue=SecurePass123! \
        ParameterKey=InstanceType,ParameterValue=t3.medium \
    --capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Framework,Value=Django
Output: { "StackId": "arn:aws:cloudformation:us-east-1:123456789012:stack/anihpj-production/abc123" }
Django Context: # Stack provisions for Django: # - VPC with public/private subnets # - RDS PostgreSQL for JobPost, Skills tables # - EC2 instances running Django # - ALB for load balancing
Example 2: CloudFormation template for Django

Infrastructure as Code template for the application.

# infrastructure.yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: 'anihpj Django Application Infrastructure'

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    AllowedValues: [production, staging, development]
  DBPassword:
    Type: String
    NoEcho: true
  InstanceType:
    Type: String
    Default: t3.medium

Resources:
  # VPC Configuration
  VPC:
    Type: AWS::EC2::VPC
    Properties:
      CidrBlock: 10.0.0.0/16
      EnableDnsHostnames: true
      Tags:
        - Key: Name
          Value: !Sub ${Environment}-anihpj-vpc

  # RDS PostgreSQL Database
  Database:
    Type: AWS::RDS::DBInstance
    Properties:
      DBInstanceIdentifier: !Sub anihpj-${Environment}-db
      DBInstanceClass: db.t3.medium
      Engine: postgres
      EngineVersion: '15.4'
      MasterUsername: anihpj_admin
      MasterUserPassword: !Ref DBPassword
      AllocatedStorage: 100
      StorageType: gp3
      VPCSecurityGroups:
        - !Ref DBSecurityGroup

  # S3 Bucket for Static Files
  StaticBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      BucketName: !Sub anihpj-${Environment}-static
      PublicAccessBlockConfiguration:
        BlockPublicAcls: false

Outputs:
  DatabaseEndpoint:
    Value: !GetAtt Database.Endpoint.Address
    Export:
      Name: !Sub ${Environment}-DBEndpoint
  StaticBucketName:
    Value: !Ref StaticBucket
Output: # Template creates: # - VPC: 10.0.0.0/16 network # - RDS: PostgreSQL 15.4 database # - S3: Static files bucket
Django Context: # Database for Django models: # - JobPost table # - Skills, Author, Location, User tables # - 100GB storage for growing data

describe-stacks

Returns information about stacks.

Example 3: Get stack status and outputs

Check the status of the Django infrastructure stack.

aws cloudformation describe-stacks \
    --stack-name anihpj-production
Output: { "Stacks": [ { "StackName": "anihpj-production", "StackId": "arn:aws:cloudformation:us-east-1:123456789012:stack/anihpj-production/abc123", "StackStatus": "CREATE_COMPLETE", "CreationTime": "2025-01-17T10:00:00.000Z", "Parameters": [ {"ParameterKey": "Environment", "ParameterValue": "production"}, {"ParameterKey": "InstanceType", "ParameterValue": "t3.medium"} ], "Outputs": [ { "OutputKey": "DatabaseEndpoint", "OutputValue": "anihpj-production-db.abc123.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com", "ExportName": "production-DBEndpoint" }, { "OutputKey": "StaticBucketName", "OutputValue": "anihpj-production-static" } ] } ] }
Django Context: # Use outputs in Django settings: # DATABASES['default']['HOST'] = 'anihpj-production-db.abc123...' # AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = 'anihpj-production-static'
Example 4: Describe stack events

View creation progress and events.

aws cloudformation describe-stack-events \
    --stack-name anihpj-production \
    --max-items 10
Output: { "StackEvents": [ { "StackId": "arn:aws:cloudformation:...", "EventId": "event-abc123", "StackName": "anihpj-production", "LogicalResourceId": "anihpj-production", "ResourceType": "AWS::CloudFormation::Stack", "Timestamp": "2025-01-17T10:15:00.000Z", "ResourceStatus": "CREATE_COMPLETE" }, { "LogicalResourceId": "Database", "ResourceType": "AWS::RDS::DBInstance", "Timestamp": "2025-01-17T10:14:00.000Z", "ResourceStatus": "CREATE_COMPLETE" }, { "LogicalResourceId": "StaticBucket", "ResourceType": "AWS::S3::Bucket", "Timestamp": "2025-01-17T10:05:00.000Z", "ResourceStatus": "CREATE_COMPLETE" } ] }
Django Context: # Resources created: # - RDS for JobPost database # - S3 for static/media files # - All resources ready for Django
Example 5: Update stack with new configuration

Update stack to increase database size.

aws cloudformation update-stack \
    --stack-name anihpj-production \
    --use-previous-template \
    --parameters \
        ParameterKey=Environment,UsePreviousValue=true \
        ParameterKey=DBPassword,UsePreviousValue=true \
        ParameterKey=InstanceType,ParameterValue=t3.large \
    --capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM
Output: { "StackId": "arn:aws:cloudformation:us-east-1:123456789012:stack/anihpj-production/abc123" }
Django Context: # Stack update: # - Upgraded instances to t3.large # - More CPU/memory for JobPost queries # - Handles more concurrent Skills searches

AWS Systems Manager

Operational management for AWS resources

📋 Systems Manager Overview

AWS Systems Manager provides operational management capabilities for AWS resources. For the anihpj Django application, Systems Manager stores configuration parameters (database credentials, API keys), manages Django server instances, and runs operational commands like migrations and cache clearing.

Core Systems Manager Commands

put-parameter - Store parameter
get-parameter - Get parameter value
get-parameters-by-path - Get multiple params
send-command - Run remote command
describe-instance-information - List instances
start-session - Start Session Manager

put-parameter

Stores a configuration parameter securely.

Syntax
aws ssm put-parameter \
    --name <parameter-name> \
    --value <value> \
    --type String|SecureString|StringList \
    [--description <description>] \
    [--overwrite]
Example 1: Store Django database credentials

Securely store database password in Parameter Store.

aws ssm put-parameter \
    --name "/anihpj/production/database/password" \
    --value "SecureDbPassword123!" \
    --type SecureString \
    --description "RDS password for anihpj Django production database" \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Environment,Value=production
Output: { "Version": 1, "Tier": "Standard" }
Django Context: # In Django settings.py: # import boto3 # ssm = boto3.client('ssm') # DB_PASSWORD = ssm.get_parameter( # Name='/anihpj/production/database/password', # WithDecryption=True # )['Parameter']['Value']
Example 2: Store all Django config parameters

Store application configuration in Parameter Store.

# Store multiple parameters
aws ssm put-parameter --name "/anihpj/production/django/secret_key" \
    --value "your-secret-key-here" --type SecureString

aws ssm put-parameter --name "/anihpj/production/django/allowed_hosts" \
    --value "anihpj.com,www.anihpj.com,api.anihpj.com" --type StringList

aws ssm put-parameter --name "/anihpj/production/aws/s3_bucket" \
    --value "anihpj-production-static" --type String

aws ssm put-parameter --name "/anihpj/production/email/smtp_password" \
    --value "smtp-password-here" --type SecureString
Output: # Multiple parameters created: /anihpj/production/django/secret_key - Version 1 /anihpj/production/django/allowed_hosts - Version 1 /anihpj/production/aws/s3_bucket - Version 1 /anihpj/production/email/smtp_password - Version 1
Django Context: # Parameters configure Django: # SECRET_KEY for session encryption # ALLOWED_HOSTS for security # S3 bucket for static files # Email for Author notifications

get-parameters-by-path

Retrieves all parameters under a path.

Example 3: Get all Django configuration

Retrieve all parameters for the Django application.

aws ssm get-parameters-by-path \
    --path "/anihpj/production" \
    --recursive \
    --with-decryption
Output: { "Parameters": [ { "Name": "/anihpj/production/database/password", "Type": "SecureString", "Value": "SecureDbPassword123!", "Version": 1 }, { "Name": "/anihpj/production/django/secret_key", "Type": "SecureString", "Value": "your-secret-key-here", "Version": 1 }, { "Name": "/anihpj/production/django/allowed_hosts", "Type": "StringList", "Value": "anihpj.com,www.anihpj.com,api.anihpj.com", "Version": 1 }, { "Name": "/anihpj/production/aws/s3_bucket", "Type": "String", "Value": "anihpj-production-static", "Version": 1 } ] }
Django Context: # All config for Django app: # - Database credentials # - Security settings # - AWS integration # - Email configuration

send-command

Runs commands on managed instances.

Example 4: Run Django migrations remotely

Execute migrations on all Django servers.

aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
    --targets Key=tag:App,Values=anihpj \
    --parameters 'commands=["cd /var/www/anihpj","source venv/bin/activate","python manage.py migrate --noinput"]' \
    --comment "Run Django migrations for JobPost model update"
Output: { "Command": { "CommandId": "cmd-0abc123def456", "DocumentName": "AWS-RunShellScript", "Comment": "Run Django migrations for JobPost model update", "Status": "Pending", "RequestedDateTime": "2025-01-17T10:30:00.000Z", "TargetCount": 3, "CompletedCount": 0 } }
Django Context: # Runs migrations on all instances: # - Applies JobPost schema changes # - Updates Skills table structure # - Adds new Author fields
Example 5: Clear Django cache on all servers

Clear cache after deployment.

aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
    --targets Key=tag:App,Values=anihpj Key=tag:Environment,Values=production \
    --parameters 'commands=["cd /var/www/anihpj","source venv/bin/activate","python manage.py clearcache","sudo systemctl restart gunicorn"]' \
    --comment "Clear cache and restart after v2.1.0 deployment"
Output: { "Command": { "CommandId": "cmd-0xyz789abc", "DocumentName": "AWS-RunShellScript", "Comment": "Clear cache and restart after v2.1.0 deployment", "Status": "Pending", "TargetCount": 3 } }
Django Context: # Post-deployment tasks: # - Clear cached JobPost querysets # - Clear Skills filter cache # - Restart Django to load new code

AWS CloudTrail

Audit logging for AWS API activity

📋 CloudTrail Overview

AWS CloudTrail records AWS API calls and events for auditing and compliance. For the anihpj Django application, CloudTrail logs all AWS operations including S3 access, RDS changes, IAM modifications, and infrastructure updates, providing a complete audit trail for security and compliance requirements.

Core CloudTrail Commands

create-trail - Create audit trail
start-logging - Enable logging
lookup-events - Search events
describe-trails - List trails
get-trail-status - Get trail status
put-event-selectors - Configure events

create-trail

Creates a new CloudTrail trail for logging.

Syntax
aws cloudtrail create-trail \
    --name <trail-name> \
    --s3-bucket-name <bucket> \
    [--is-multi-region-trail] \
    [--enable-log-file-validation] \
    [--kms-key-id <key-id>]
Example 1: Create audit trail for anihpj

Create a CloudTrail for auditing all AWS operations.

aws cloudtrail create-trail \
    --name anihpj-audit-trail \
    --s3-bucket-name anihpj-cloudtrail-logs \
    --is-multi-region-trail \
    --enable-log-file-validation \
    --include-global-service-events \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Purpose,Value=Audit
Output: { "Name": "anihpj-audit-trail", "S3BucketName": "anihpj-cloudtrail-logs", "IncludeGlobalServiceEvents": true, "IsMultiRegionTrail": true, "TrailARN": "arn:aws:cloudtrail:us-east-1:123456789012:trail/anihpj-audit-trail", "LogFileValidationEnabled": true, "IsOrganizationTrail": false }
Django Context: # Tracks AWS operations for Django: # - S3 access for static files # - RDS database modifications # - EC2 instance changes # - IAM permission updates

lookup-events

Searches for management events in CloudTrail.

Example 2: Find S3 bucket access events

Look up who accessed the Django static files bucket.

aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
    --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=ResourceName,AttributeValue=anihpj-production-static \
    --start-time 2025-01-16T00:00:00Z \
    --end-time 2025-01-17T23:59:59Z
Output: { "Events": [ { "EventId": "event-abc123", "EventName": "PutObject", "EventTime": "2025-01-17T10:30:00Z", "EventSource": "s3.amazonaws.com", "Username": "codebuild-service-role", "Resources": [ { "ResourceType": "AWS::S3::Bucket", "ResourceName": "anihpj-production-static" } ], "CloudTrailEvent": "{\"eventVersion\":\"1.08\",\"eventSource\":\"s3.amazonaws.com\"...}" }, { "EventId": "event-def456", "EventName": "GetObject", "EventTime": "2025-01-17T11:00:00Z", "EventSource": "s3.amazonaws.com", "Username": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/django-ec2-role" } ] }
Django Context: # S3 access audit: # - CodeBuild uploaded static files # - Django EC2 retrieved files # - Track who accessed media uploads
Example 3: Find RDS database events

Look up database modifications.

aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
    --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=EventSource,AttributeValue=rds.amazonaws.com \
    --start-time 2025-01-17T00:00:00Z \
    --max-results 10
Output: { "Events": [ { "EventId": "event-rds123", "EventName": "ModifyDBInstance", "EventTime": "2025-01-17T09:00:00Z", "EventSource": "rds.amazonaws.com", "Username": "admin-user", "Resources": [ { "ResourceType": "AWS::RDS::DBInstance", "ResourceName": "anihpj-production-db" } ] }, { "EventId": "event-rds456", "EventName": "CreateDBSnapshot", "EventTime": "2025-01-17T03:00:00Z", "EventSource": "rds.amazonaws.com", "Username": "aws-backup-service" } ] }
Django Context: # Database audit trail: # - Who modified RDS settings # - Automated backup snapshots # - Changes affecting JobPost queries
Example 4: Find IAM changes

Audit IAM permission modifications.

aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
    --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=EventSource,AttributeValue=iam.amazonaws.com \
    --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=EventName,AttributeValue=AttachRolePolicy
Output: { "Events": [ { "EventId": "event-iam123", "EventName": "AttachRolePolicy", "EventTime": "2025-01-17T08:00:00Z", "EventSource": "iam.amazonaws.com", "Username": "security-admin", "Resources": [ { "ResourceType": "AWS::IAM::Role", "ResourceName": "django-ec2-role" }, { "ResourceType": "AWS::IAM::Policy", "ResourceName": "AmazonS3FullAccess" } ] } ] }
Django Context: # IAM audit for security: # - Track permission changes # - Django EC2 role modifications # - Ensure least privilege access
Example 5: Get trail status

Check if CloudTrail is actively logging.

aws cloudtrail get-trail-status \
    --name anihpj-audit-trail
Output: { "IsLogging": true, "LatestDeliveryTime": "2025-01-17T12:45:00.000Z", "StartLoggingTime": "2025-01-01T00:00:00.000Z", "LatestNotificationTime": "2025-01-17T12:45:00.000Z", "LatestDeliveryAttemptTime": "2025-01-17T12:45:00.000Z", "LatestNotificationAttemptSucceeded": "2025-01-17T12:45:00.000Z", "LatestDeliveryAttemptSucceeded": "2025-01-17T12:45:00.000Z", "TimeLoggingStarted": "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z", "TimeLoggingStopped": "" }
Django Context: # Trail actively logging: # - All AWS API calls recorded # - Logs delivered to S3 bucket # - Complete audit for compliance

🐳 Containers

AWS Container services provide fully managed container orchestration and registry. This section covers ECS for container orchestration, EKS for Kubernetes, and ECR for container registry. Learn how to deploy your anihpj Django application in containers.

Amazon ECS

Fully managed container orchestration service

📋 ECS Overview

Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service) is a fully managed container orchestration service. For the anihpj Django application, ECS runs Docker containers hosting the Django web server, Celery workers for async tasks, and scheduled jobs for JobPost, Skills, Author, and Location operations.

Core ECS Commands

create-cluster - Create cluster
create-service - Create service
run-task - Run one-off task
describe-services - Get service info
update-service - Update service
list-tasks - List running tasks

create-cluster

Creates a new ECS cluster for running containers.

Syntax
aws ecs create-cluster \
    --cluster-name <name> \
    [--capacity-providers <providers>] \
    [--settings name=containerInsights,value=enabled]
Example 1: Create Django cluster

Create an ECS cluster for the Django application.

aws ecs create-cluster \
    --cluster-name anihpj-cluster \
    --capacity-providers FARGATE FARGATE_SPOT \
    --default-capacity-provider-strategy capacityProvider=FARGATE,weight=1,base=1 \
    --settings name=containerInsights,value=enabled \
    --tags key=Project,value=anihpj key=Framework,value=Django
Output: { "cluster": { "clusterArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-cluster", "clusterName": "anihpj-cluster", "status": "ACTIVE", "registeredContainerInstancesCount": 0, "runningTasksCount": 0, "capacityProviders": ["FARGATE", "FARGATE_SPOT"], "settings": [ {"name": "containerInsights", "value": "enabled"} ] } }
Django Context: # Cluster runs Django containers: # - Web servers for JobPost API # - Celery workers for async tasks # - Container Insights for monitoring

create-service

Creates a service to run and maintain tasks.

Example 2: Create Django web service

Create an ECS service for Django web containers.

aws ecs create-service \
    --cluster anihpj-cluster \
    --service-name anihpj-web \
    --task-definition anihpj-django-web:3 \
    --desired-count 3 \
    --launch-type FARGATE \
    --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-abc123,subnet-def456],securityGroups=[sg-web123],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}" \
    --load-balancers targetGroupArn=arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/anihpj-tg/abc123,containerName=django-web,containerPort=8000 \
    --deployment-configuration minimumHealthyPercent=50,maximumPercent=200
Output: { "service": { "serviceArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:service/anihpj-cluster/anihpj-web", "serviceName": "anihpj-web", "clusterArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-cluster", "desiredCount": 3, "runningCount": 0, "pendingCount": 3, "launchType": "FARGATE", "status": "ACTIVE" } }
Django Context: # 3 containers serving Django: # - Load balanced for JobPost API # - Auto-replaced if unhealthy # - Rolling deploys for zero downtime
Example 3: Run Django migration task

Run one-off task for database migrations.

aws ecs run-task \
    --cluster anihpj-cluster \
    --task-definition anihpj-django-web:3 \
    --launch-type FARGATE \
    --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-abc123],securityGroups=[sg-db123],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}" \
    --overrides '{
        "containerOverrides": [{
            "name": "django-web",
            "command": ["python", "manage.py", "migrate", "--noinput"]
        }]
    }'
Output: { "tasks": [ { "taskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/anihpj-cluster/abc123def456", "clusterArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-cluster", "taskDefinitionArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-django-web:3", "lastStatus": "PENDING", "desiredStatus": "RUNNING", "launchType": "FARGATE" } ] }
Django Context: # Migration task applies: # - JobPost model changes # - Skills table updates # - Author/Location field additions
Example 4: Describe service status

Get current status of the Django web service.

aws ecs describe-services \
    --cluster anihpj-cluster \
    --services anihpj-web
Output: { "services": [ { "serviceName": "anihpj-web", "status": "ACTIVE", "desiredCount": 3, "runningCount": 3, "pendingCount": 0, "launchType": "FARGATE", "taskDefinition": "arn:aws:ecs:...:task-definition/anihpj-django-web:3", "deployments": [ { "id": "ecs-svc/abc123", "status": "PRIMARY", "taskDefinition": "arn:aws:ecs:...:task-definition/anihpj-django-web:3", "desiredCount": 3, "runningCount": 3, "rolloutState": "COMPLETED" } ], "events": [ { "createdAt": "2025-01-17T10:30:00.000Z", "message": "(service anihpj-web) has reached a steady state." } ] } ] }
Django Context: # Service healthy: # - 3/3 Django containers running # - Deployment completed successfully # - Serving JobPost, Skills APIs
Example 5: Update service to new version

Deploy new Django version with rolling update.

aws ecs update-service \
    --cluster anihpj-cluster \
    --service anihpj-web \
    --task-definition anihpj-django-web:4 \
    --force-new-deployment
Output: { "service": { "serviceName": "anihpj-web", "taskDefinition": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-django-web:4", "desiredCount": 3, "runningCount": 3, "deployments": [ { "id": "ecs-svc/new123", "status": "PRIMARY", "taskDefinition": "...:task-definition/anihpj-django-web:4", "desiredCount": 3, "runningCount": 0, "rolloutState": "IN_PROGRESS" }, { "id": "ecs-svc/old456", "status": "ACTIVE", "taskDefinition": "...:task-definition/anihpj-django-web:3", "desiredCount": 0, "runningCount": 3 } ] } }
Django Context: # Rolling deployment: # - New containers starting (v4) # - Old containers draining (v3) # - Zero downtime for JobPost users

Amazon EKS

Managed Kubernetes service for container orchestration

📋 EKS Overview

Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) is a managed Kubernetes service for running containerized applications. For the anihpj Django application, EKS provides Kubernetes-based orchestration for Django pods, Celery workers, Redis caching, and microservices handling JobPost, Skills, Author, and Location operations.

Core EKS Commands

create-cluster - Create K8s cluster
create-nodegroup - Add worker nodes
describe-cluster - Get cluster info
update-kubeconfig - Configure kubectl
list-clusters - List clusters
delete-cluster - Delete cluster

create-cluster

Creates a new EKS Kubernetes cluster.

Syntax
aws eks create-cluster \
    --name <cluster-name> \
    --role-arn <role-arn> \
    --resources-vpc-config subnetIds=<subnets>,securityGroupIds=<sgs> \
    [--kubernetes-version <version>]
Example 1: Create Kubernetes cluster

Create an EKS cluster for the Django application.

aws eks create-cluster \
    --name anihpj-k8s \
    --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/EKSClusterRole \
    --resources-vpc-config subnetIds=subnet-abc123,subnet-def456,subnet-ghi789,securityGroupIds=sg-eks123 \
    --kubernetes-version 1.28 \
    --tags Project=anihpj,Framework=Django
Output: { "cluster": { "name": "anihpj-k8s", "arn": "arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-k8s", "version": "1.28", "status": "CREATING", "roleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/EKSClusterRole", "resourcesVpcConfig": { "subnetIds": ["subnet-abc123", "subnet-def456", "subnet-ghi789"], "securityGroupIds": ["sg-eks123"], "endpointPublicAccess": true, "endpointPrivateAccess": false }, "platformVersion": "eks.5" } }
Django Context: # Kubernetes cluster for Django: # - Control plane managed by AWS # - Runs Django, Celery, Redis pods # - Kubernetes 1.28 for latest features

create-nodegroup

Creates a managed node group for worker nodes.

Example 2: Create worker node group

Add worker nodes to run Django pods.

aws eks create-nodegroup \
    --cluster-name anihpj-k8s \
    --nodegroup-name anihpj-workers \
    --node-role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/EKSNodeRole \
    --subnets subnet-abc123 subnet-def456 \
    --instance-types t3.medium t3.large \
    --scaling-config minSize=2,maxSize=10,desiredSize=3 \
    --capacity-type ON_DEMAND \
    --labels app=anihpj,tier=web
Output: { "nodegroup": { "nodegroupName": "anihpj-workers", "nodegroupArn": "arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:123456789012:nodegroup/anihpj-k8s/anihpj-workers/abc123", "clusterName": "anihpj-k8s", "status": "CREATING", "scalingConfig": { "minSize": 2, "maxSize": 10, "desiredSize": 3 }, "instanceTypes": ["t3.medium", "t3.large"], "capacityType": "ON_DEMAND" } }
Django Context: # Worker nodes run Django pods: # - 3 nodes for Django web deployments # - Auto-scale to 10 for traffic spikes # - Handles JobPost API load
Example 3: Configure kubectl

Update kubeconfig to access the cluster.

aws eks update-kubeconfig \
    --name anihpj-k8s \
    --region us-east-1
Output: Added new context arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-k8s to /home/user/.kube/config
Django Context: # kubectl now connected to cluster # Can deploy Django manifests: # kubectl apply -f django-deployment.yaml # kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
Example 4: Describe cluster

Get details of the Kubernetes cluster.

aws eks describe-cluster \
    --name anihpj-k8s
Output: { "cluster": { "name": "anihpj-k8s", "arn": "arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-k8s", "version": "1.28", "status": "ACTIVE", "endpoint": "https://ABC123DEF.gr7.us-east-1.eks.amazonaws.com", "certificateAuthority": { "data": "LS0tLS1CRUdJTiBDRVJUSUZ..." }, "platformVersion": "eks.5", "health": { "issues": [] } } }
Django Context: # Cluster ready for Django: # - API endpoint available # - Certificate for secure access # - No health issues
Example 5: Django Kubernetes deployment manifest

Kubernetes deployment for Django application.

# django-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-django
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    tier: web
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      tier: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        tier: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings.production"
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secrets
              key: database-url
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /api/health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: anihpj
    tier: web
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  type: LoadBalancer
Output: # kubectl apply -f django-deployment.yaml deployment.apps/anihpj-django created service/anihpj-service created # kubectl get pods NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-django-7d9f8b5c6-abc12 1/1 Running 0 2m anihpj-django-7d9f8b5c6-def34 1/1 Running 0 2m anihpj-django-7d9f8b5c6-ghi56 1/1 Running 0 2m
Django Context: # 3 Django pods serving: # - JobPost.objects.all() API # - Skills.objects.filter() search # - Author/Location queries # - LoadBalancer exposes service

Amazon ECR

Fully managed container registry for Docker images

📋 ECR Overview

Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) is a fully managed Docker container registry. For the anihpj Django application, ECR stores Docker images for the Django web application, Celery workers, and any other microservices needed to run JobPost, Skills, Author, and Location functionality.

Core ECR Commands

create-repository - Create repo
get-login-password - Get auth token
describe-repositories - List repos
describe-images - List images
delete-repository - Delete repo
put-lifecycle-policy - Set cleanup rules

create-repository

Creates a new ECR repository for Docker images.

Syntax
aws ecr create-repository \
    --repository-name <name> \
    [--image-scanning-configuration scanOnPush=true] \
    [--encryption-configuration encryptionType=AES256|KMS]
Example 1: Create Django image repository

Create an ECR repository for the Django application.

aws ecr create-repository \
    --repository-name anihpj/django \
    --image-scanning-configuration scanOnPush=true \
    --encryption-configuration encryptionType=AES256 \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Framework,Value=Django
Output: { "repository": { "repositoryArn": "arn:aws:ecr:us-east-1:123456789012:repository/anihpj/django", "registryId": "123456789012", "repositoryName": "anihpj/django", "repositoryUri": "123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/django", "createdAt": "2025-01-17T10:00:00.000Z", "imageTagMutability": "MUTABLE", "imageScanningConfiguration": { "scanOnPush": true }, "encryptionConfiguration": { "encryptionType": "AES256" } } }
Django Context: # Repository stores Django images: # - Base image with Python 3.11 # - Django 5.2.5 dependencies # - JobPost app code # - Auto-scans for vulnerabilities

get-login-password

Gets an authentication token for Docker login.

Example 2: Authenticate Docker with ECR

Login to ECR to push/pull images.

aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | \
    docker login --username AWS --password-stdin \
    123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
Output: Login Succeeded
Django Context: # Now can push Django images: # docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.../anihpj/django:latest # ECS/EKS can pull for deployments
Example 3: Build and push Django image

Build Django Docker image and push to ECR.

# Dockerfile for Django
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy Django project
COPY . .

# Collect static files
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

EXPOSE 8000

CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]

# Build and push
docker build -t anihpj/django:v2.1.0 .
docker tag anihpj/django:v2.1.0 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/django:v2.1.0
docker tag anihpj/django:v2.1.0 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/django:latest
docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/django:v2.1.0
docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/django:latest
Output: The push refers to repository [123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/django] abc123: Pushed def456: Pushed ghi789: Pushed v2.1.0: digest: sha256:abc123def456... size: 1234 latest: digest: sha256:abc123def456... size: 1234
Django Context: # Image contains: # - Python 3.11, Django 5.2.5 # - JobPost, Skills, Author, Location models # - Gunicorn for production serving
Example 4: Describe images in repository

List all images with their tags and digests.

aws ecr describe-images \
    --repository-name anihpj/django \
    --query 'imageDetails[*].{Tags:imageTags,Pushed:imagePushedAt,Size:imageSizeInBytes}'
Output: [ { "Tags": ["v2.1.0", "latest"], "Pushed": "2025-01-17T10:30:00.000Z", "Size": 245678901 }, { "Tags": ["v2.0.0"], "Pushed": "2025-01-15T14:00:00.000Z", "Size": 243567890 }, { "Tags": ["v1.9.0"], "Pushed": "2025-01-10T09:00:00.000Z", "Size": 240123456 } ]
Django Context: # Image versions: # - v2.1.0: Skills.level field added # - v2.0.0: JobPost status workflow # - v1.9.0: Author bio field # ECS/EKS can deploy any version
Example 5: Set image lifecycle policy

Configure automatic cleanup of old images.

aws ecr put-lifecycle-policy \
    --repository-name anihpj/django \
    --lifecycle-policy-text '{
        "rules": [
            {
                "rulePriority": 1,
                "description": "Keep last 10 images",
                "selection": {
                    "tagStatus": "tagged",
                    "tagPrefixList": ["v"],
                    "countType": "imageCountMoreThan",
                    "countNumber": 10
                },
                "action": {
                    "type": "expire"
                }
            },
            {
                "rulePriority": 2,
                "description": "Delete untagged images older than 7 days",
                "selection": {
                    "tagStatus": "untagged",
                    "countType": "sinceImagePushed",
                    "countUnit": "days",
                    "countNumber": 7
                },
                "action": {
                    "type": "expire"
                }
            }
        ]
    }'
Output: { "registryId": "123456789012", "repositoryName": "anihpj/django", "lifecyclePolicyText": "{...}" }
Django Context: # Lifecycle policy: # - Keep 10 most recent versions # - Delete untagged after 7 days # - Saves storage costs # - Maintains rollback capability

🔒 Security & Identity

AWS Security services help you secure your applications and data. This section covers IAM for identity and access management, Cognito for user authentication, Certificate Manager for SSL/TLS, Secrets Manager for credential management, and KMS for encryption key management.

AWS IAM

Identity and Access Management for AWS resources

📋 IAM Overview

AWS IAM (Identity and Access Management) enables you to manage access to AWS services securely. For the anihpj Django application, IAM controls access to S3 buckets, RDS databases, Lambda functions, and other AWS resources that support JobPost, Skills, Author, and Location operations.

Core IAM Commands

create-role - Create IAM role
create-policy - Create policy
attach-role-policy - Attach policy to role
create-user - Create IAM user
list-roles - List all roles
get-role - Get role details

create-role

Creates a new IAM role for AWS services.

Syntax
aws iam create-role \
    --role-name <name> \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://<trust-policy.json> \
    [--description <description>] \
    [--tags Key=<key>,Value=<value>]
Example 1: Create EC2 role for Django

Create an IAM role for EC2 instances running Django.

# trust-policy.json
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"
            },
            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
        }
    ]
}

aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-django-ec2-role \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://trust-policy.json \
    --description "IAM role for Django EC2 instances" \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Framework,Value=Django
Output: { "Role": { "RoleName": "anihpj-django-ec2-role", "RoleId": "AROAEXAMPLEID123", "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-django-ec2-role", "CreateDate": "2025-01-17T10:00:00.000Z", "AssumeRolePolicyDocument": {...} } }
Django Context: # EC2 role allows Django to: # - Access S3 for static files # - Read Secrets Manager for DB password # - Publish to CloudWatch for metrics

create-policy

Creates a new IAM policy with specified permissions.

Example 2: Create S3 access policy

Create policy for Django to access S3 buckets.

# s3-policy.json
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "StaticFilesAccess",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Action": [
                "s3:GetObject",
                "s3:PutObject",
                "s3:DeleteObject",
                "s3:ListBucket"
            ],
            "Resource": [
                "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-static-files",
                "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-static-files/*",
                "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-media-uploads",
                "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-media-uploads/*"
            ]
        }
    ]
}

aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-s3-access \
    --policy-document file://s3-policy.json \
    --description "S3 access for Django static and media files"
Output: { "Policy": { "PolicyName": "anihpj-s3-access", "PolicyId": "ANPAEXAMPLEID456", "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-s3-access", "CreateDate": "2025-01-17T10:05:00.000Z", "DefaultVersionId": "v1" } }
Django Context: # Policy permits Django: # - Upload JobPost attachments # - Serve Author profile images # - Store collected static files

attach-role-policy

Attaches a managed policy to an IAM role.

Example 3: Attach policies to Django role

Attach S3 and Secrets Manager policies to Django role.

# Attach S3 policy
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-django-ec2-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-s3-access

# Attach Secrets Manager read-only
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-django-ec2-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/SecretsManagerReadWrite

# Attach CloudWatch for metrics
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-django-ec2-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/CloudWatchAgentServerPolicy
Output: # No output on success (HTTP 200)
Django Context: # Django EC2 now has permissions: # - S3 for media (Author images, JobPost files) # - Secrets Manager for DB credentials # - CloudWatch for application metrics
Example 4: Create Lambda execution role

Create role for Lambda functions processing JobPost events.

# lambda-trust-policy.json
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {"Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com"},
            "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
        }
    ]
}

aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-lambda-role \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://lambda-trust-policy.json

# Attach basic execution and SQS access
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-lambda-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole

aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-lambda-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonSQSFullAccess
Output: { "Role": { "RoleName": "anihpj-lambda-role", "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda-role" } }
Django Context: # Lambda role for processing: # - JobPost creation events # - Skills validation requests # - SQS queue access for async tasks
Example 5: List roles for anihpj

List all IAM roles for the Django application.

aws iam list-roles \
    --query "Roles[?contains(RoleName, 'anihpj')]"
Output: [ { "RoleName": "anihpj-django-ec2-role", "RoleId": "AROAEXAMPLEID123", "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-django-ec2-role", "CreateDate": "2025-01-17T10:00:00.000Z" }, { "RoleName": "anihpj-lambda-role", "RoleId": "AROAEXAMPLEID456", "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda-role", "CreateDate": "2025-01-17T10:10:00.000Z" }, { "RoleName": "anihpj-ecs-task-role", "RoleId": "AROAEXAMPLEID789", "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-ecs-task-role", "CreateDate": "2025-01-16T14:00:00.000Z" } ]
Django Context: # Roles supporting Django: # - EC2 role for web servers # - Lambda role for serverless functions # - ECS task role for containers # All follow least privilege principle

AWS Cognito

User authentication and authorization service

📋 Cognito Overview

AWS Cognito provides authentication, authorization, and user management for web and mobile applications. For the anihpj Django application, Cognito handles User registration, Author authentication, social login integration, and secure access to JobPost creation and Skills management features.

Core Cognito Commands

create-user-pool - Create user pool
create-user-pool-client - Create app client
admin-create-user - Create user
admin-get-user - Get user details
list-user-pools - List pools
create-identity-pool - Create identity pool

create-user-pool

Creates a new Cognito user pool for authentication.

Syntax
aws cognito-idp create-user-pool \
    --pool-name <name> \
    [--policies PasswordPolicy={...}] \
    [--auto-verified-attributes <attributes>] \
    [--schema <custom-attributes>]
Example 1: Create user pool for Django auth

Create a Cognito user pool for anihpj users.

aws cognito-idp create-user-pool \
    --pool-name anihpj-users \
    --policies 'PasswordPolicy={MinimumLength=8,RequireUppercase=true,RequireLowercase=true,RequireNumbers=true,RequireSymbols=false}' \
    --auto-verified-attributes email \
    --username-attributes email \
    --schema '[{"Name":"author_id","AttributeDataType":"Number","Mutable":true},{"Name":"is_employer","AttributeDataType":"Boolean","Mutable":true}]' \
    --mfa-configuration OFF
Output: { "UserPool": { "Id": "us-east-1_ABc123XyZ", "Name": "anihpj-users", "Policies": { "PasswordPolicy": { "MinimumLength": 8, "RequireUppercase": true, "RequireLowercase": true, "RequireNumbers": true } }, "AutoVerifiedAttributes": ["email"], "UsernameAttributes": ["email"], "SchemaAttributes": [ {"Name": "email", "AttributeDataType": "String", "Required": true}, {"Name": "custom:author_id", "AttributeDataType": "Number"}, {"Name": "custom:is_employer", "AttributeDataType": "Boolean"} ], "CreationDate": "2025-01-17T10:00:00.000Z", "Arn": "arn:aws:cognito-idp:us-east-1:123456789012:userpool/us-east-1_ABc123XyZ" } }
Django Context: # User pool stores Django users: # - Links to User.objects via author_id # - is_employer flag for JobPost creation # - Email-based authentication

create-user-pool-client

Creates an application client for the user pool.

Example 2: Create app client for Django

Create a client for the Django web application.

aws cognito-idp create-user-pool-client \
    --user-pool-id us-east-1_ABc123XyZ \
    --client-name anihpj-web-client \
    --generate-secret \
    --explicit-auth-flows ALLOW_USER_PASSWORD_AUTH ALLOW_REFRESH_TOKEN_AUTH \
    --supported-identity-providers COGNITO \
    --callback-urls '["https://anihpj.com/auth/callback","http://localhost:8000/auth/callback"]' \
    --logout-urls '["https://anihpj.com/logout","http://localhost:8000/logout"]' \
    --allowed-o-auth-flows code \
    --allowed-o-auth-scopes openid email profile
Output: { "UserPoolClient": { "UserPoolId": "us-east-1_ABc123XyZ", "ClientName": "anihpj-web-client", "ClientId": "1abc2def3ghi4jkl5mno", "ClientSecret": "secret123abc456def789ghi...", "ExplicitAuthFlows": [ "ALLOW_USER_PASSWORD_AUTH", "ALLOW_REFRESH_TOKEN_AUTH" ], "CallbackURLs": [ "https://anihpj.com/auth/callback", "http://localhost:8000/auth/callback" ] } }
Django Context: # Django uses this client for: # - User login/registration # - Author account creation # - OAuth flow for social login # Store ClientId/Secret in Django settings

admin-create-user

Creates a new user in the user pool.

Example 3: Create admin user

Create an admin user for JobPost management.

aws cognito-idp admin-create-user \
    --user-pool-id us-east-1_ABc123XyZ \
    --username admin@anihpj.com \
    --user-attributes Name=email,Value=admin@anihpj.com Name=email_verified,Value=true Name=custom:is_employer,Value=true \
    --temporary-password "TempPass123!" \
    --message-action SUPPRESS
Output: { "User": { "Username": "admin@anihpj.com", "Attributes": [ {"Name": "email", "Value": "admin@anihpj.com"}, {"Name": "email_verified", "Value": "true"}, {"Name": "custom:is_employer", "Value": "true"} ], "UserCreateDate": "2025-01-17T10:15:00.000Z", "UserStatus": "FORCE_CHANGE_PASSWORD", "Enabled": true } }
Django Context: # Admin user syncs with Django: # User.objects.create(email='admin@anihpj.com', is_staff=True) # Author.objects.create(user=user, is_employer=True) # Can create and manage JobPost entries
Example 4: Get user details

Retrieve user information from Cognito.

aws cognito-idp admin-get-user \
    --user-pool-id us-east-1_ABc123XyZ \
    --username admin@anihpj.com
Output: { "Username": "admin@anihpj.com", "UserAttributes": [ {"Name": "sub", "Value": "abc123-def456-ghi789"}, {"Name": "email", "Value": "admin@anihpj.com"}, {"Name": "email_verified", "Value": "true"}, {"Name": "custom:author_id", "Value": "67"}, {"Name": "custom:is_employer", "Value": "true"} ], "UserCreateDate": "2025-01-17T10:15:00.000Z", "UserLastModifiedDate": "2025-01-17T10:30:00.000Z", "Enabled": true, "UserStatus": "CONFIRMED" }
Django Context: # Maps to Django models: # - sub: Cognito unique ID # - author_id: Author.objects.get(pk=67) # - is_employer: Can create JobPost # Used for API authentication
Example 5: List users with Skills filter

List users who are employers.

aws cognito-idp list-users \
    --user-pool-id us-east-1_ABc123XyZ \
    --filter 'cognito:user_status = "CONFIRMED"' \
    --limit 20
Output: { "Users": [ { "Username": "admin@anihpj.com", "Attributes": [ {"Name": "email", "Value": "admin@anihpj.com"}, {"Name": "custom:is_employer", "Value": "true"} ], "UserStatus": "CONFIRMED", "Enabled": true }, { "Username": "employer1@company.com", "Attributes": [ {"Name": "email", "Value": "employer1@company.com"}, {"Name": "custom:is_employer", "Value": "true"} ], "UserStatus": "CONFIRMED", "Enabled": true }, { "Username": "jobseeker@email.com", "Attributes": [ {"Name": "email", "Value": "jobseeker@email.com"}, {"Name": "custom:is_employer", "Value": "false"} ], "UserStatus": "CONFIRMED", "Enabled": true } ] }
Django Context: # Cognito users correspond to: # User.objects.filter(is_active=True) # Author.objects.filter(is_employer=True) can create jobs # JobPost.author links to Cognito user

AWS Certificate Manager

Managed SSL/TLS certificates for AWS resources

📋 ACM Overview

AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) handles the complexity of creating, storing, and renewing SSL/TLS certificates. For the anihpj Django application, ACM provides HTTPS certificates for the Application Load Balancer, CloudFront distributions, and API Gateway endpoints serving JobPost, Skills, and Author data.

Core ACM Commands

request-certificate - Request certificate
describe-certificate - Get cert details
list-certificates - List all certs
add-tags-to-certificate - Add tags
delete-certificate - Delete cert
export-certificate - Export cert

request-certificate

Requests a new SSL/TLS certificate.

Syntax
aws acm request-certificate \
    --domain-name <domain> \
    [--subject-alternative-names <domains>] \
    --validation-method DNS|EMAIL \
    [--tags Key=<key>,Value=<value>]
Example 1: Request wildcard certificate

Request SSL certificate for all anihpj subdomains.

aws acm request-certificate \
    --domain-name anihpj.com \
    --subject-alternative-names "*.anihpj.com" "www.anihpj.com" "api.anihpj.com" \
    --validation-method DNS \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Environment,Value=production
Output: { "CertificateArn": "arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/abc123-def456-ghi789" }
Django Context: # Certificate covers Django endpoints: # - anihpj.com (main site) # - api.anihpj.com (JobPost REST API) # - www.anihpj.com (redirect) # All traffic encrypted for security

describe-certificate

Returns detailed information about a certificate.

Example 2: Get certificate status

Check the validation status of the certificate.

aws acm describe-certificate \
    --certificate-arn arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/abc123-def456-ghi789
Output: { "Certificate": { "CertificateArn": "arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/abc123-def456-ghi789", "DomainName": "anihpj.com", "SubjectAlternativeNames": [ "anihpj.com", "*.anihpj.com", "www.anihpj.com", "api.anihpj.com" ], "DomainValidationOptions": [ { "DomainName": "anihpj.com", "ValidationDomain": "anihpj.com", "ValidationStatus": "SUCCESS", "ResourceRecord": { "Name": "_abc123.anihpj.com.", "Type": "CNAME", "Value": "_xyz789.acm-validations.aws." } } ], "Status": "ISSUED", "Type": "AMAZON_ISSUED", "IssuedAt": "2025-01-17T10:15:00.000Z", "NotBefore": "2025-01-17T00:00:00.000Z", "NotAfter": "2026-01-17T23:59:59.000Z", "KeyAlgorithm": "RSA-2048", "RenewalEligibility": "ELIGIBLE" } }
Django Context: # Certificate ready for use: # - Status: ISSUED # - Valid for 1 year # - Auto-renews before expiration # Apply to ALB for Django HTTPS
Example 3: Get DNS validation records

Retrieve DNS records needed for validation.

aws acm describe-certificate \
    --certificate-arn arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/abc123-def456-ghi789 \
    --query "Certificate.DomainValidationOptions[*].ResourceRecord"
Output: [ { "Name": "_abc123def456.anihpj.com.", "Type": "CNAME", "Value": "_xyz789abc123.acm-validations.aws." } ]
Django Context: # Add this CNAME to Route 53: # Validates domain ownership # Certificate issued after validation # Then attach to ALB for Django HTTPS
Example 4: List all certificates

List certificates for the account.

aws acm list-certificates \
    --certificate-statuses ISSUED \
    --query "CertificateSummaryList[?contains(DomainName, 'anihpj')]"
